@@ -135,22 +135,21 @@ There are no concurrent writers in the same project.") protects against
 concurrent _Recipe Bottle_ operations.  However, Google's own internal

-**Accidental etag passthrough in current code.**
-The compose function (`rbgi_jq_add_member_to_role_capture`) applies

+and without notice.  This is why every grant pattern's etag handling is
+load-bearing even under single-writer: relying on the single-writer assumption
+alone would leave the read-modify-write window unguarded against these

+
+**Intentional etag extraction in current code.**
+Every grant pattern now extracts the etag from the `getIamPolicy` response and
+asserts it is non-empty (`buc_die "Empty etag"` and per-resource variants),
+then threads it through the compose function
+(`rbgi_jq_add_member_to_role_capture`), whose
+`if $etag != "" then .etag = $etag else . end` filter writes the extracted etag
+into the composed policy JSON.  The earlier behavior â€” passing empty string and
+relying on the jq filter leaving the original response etag as accidental
+passthrough â€” has been replaced by deliberate, asserted optimistic concurrency.
+The write is now intentionally concurrency-safe rather than accidentally so.

+and {rbtoe_iam_grant_sa} send `{"options":{"requestedPolicyVersion":3}}` in
+the POST body; {rbtoe_iam_grant_repo}, {rbtoe_iam_grant_secret}, and

+these resources to acquire conditioned IAM policies (via future work or
+organization-level policy inheritance), a version-1 read would mangle the
+conditions into `_withcond_` role suffixes and a subsequent `setIamPolicy`
+would destroy them.  Requesting version 3 everywhere closes that hazard.

-===== Current Code vs Actual API Contract
+===== Code Convergence onto the API Contract
 
-This section catalogs discrepancies between the current implementation and the
-verified API contracts.  These are not necessarily bugs that cause failures â€”
-several work by accident or are protected by the single-writer operational
-invariant â€” but they represent incorrect assumptions in the code.
+The implementation has converged onto the verified API contracts catalogued
+above.  This section records, per pattern, how the current code in
+`rbgi_IAM.sh` realizes the contract â€” the gaps an earlier draft of this memo
+flagged (wrong etag-mismatch code, vanilla-init on impossible responses,
+unconditional etag-skipping overwrites, missing `requestedPolicyVersion`) are
+now closed.

-|Discrepancy
-|Detail

- would mask this real error.
+|409 ABORTED on etag mismatch
+|`setIamPolicy` is POST; the SET loop dies fatally on HTTP 409
+ (`HTTP 409 ABORTED (etag mismatch â€” concurrent policy change)`).
+ CRM uses the standard IAM mixin 409, not 412 â€” the code matches.
 
 |{rbtoe_iam_grant_repo}
-|Resolved â€” uses GET per proto
-|The shared `rbgi_add_repo_iam_role` function already used GET.
- The inline director AR grant path in `rbgg_Governor.sh` was the

- (transcoding layer rejecting POST on GET-only endpoint).  Fixed to
- GET with `requestedPolicyVersion=3` query parameter.  The 404
- vanilla-init handler was also removed (404 on GET means repo does
- not exist â€” a real error, not a policy absence).
+|GET getIamPolicy, no 404 vanilla-init
+|`rbgi_add_repo_iam_role` reads via GET with the
+ `options.requestedPolicyVersion=3` query parameter and has no 404
+ vanilla-init branch.  A non-200 GET hits `rbuh_require_ok` and dies:
+ 404 means the repository does not exist (a real error), not an absent
+ policy.  The inline director AR grant path in `rbgg_Governor.sh` was
+ the historical POST-on-GET-only site (observed failing with HTTP 404

-|Skips available etag
-|Passes empty string for etag (line 315), performing unconditional
- overwrite despite the API always returning etag.
- Safe under single-writer invariant but discards protection the API

- actual behavior may be passthrough rather than omission, but this
- is accidental, not intentional.
+|Non-empty etag, threaded to SET
+|The GET response etag is extracted and asserted non-empty
+ (`buc_die "Empty repo etag"`), then passed to the compose function and
+ written into the SET body â€” intentional optimistic concurrency, not the
+ earlier empty-string passthrough.  SET dies fatally on HTTP 409.
 
 |{rbtoe_iam_grant_sa}
-|Vanilla init for impossible response
-|Code initializes vanilla policy on non-200 (line 411).
- IAM `getIamPolicy` returns 200 with empty policy for existing SAs.
- Non-200 means the SA does not exist or access denied â€” vanilla init
- masks a real error.
+|Hard SA-existence preflight, no vanilla-init
+|Before the policy read, the code does a GET on the SA resource and
+ requires HTTP 200, dying with "Target service account not accessible"
+ otherwise.  The read-modify-write then proceeds with no vanilla-init on
+ non-200; IAM `getIamPolicy` returns 200 + empty policy for existing SAs.
 
 |{rbtoe_iam_grant_sa}
-|Skips available etag
-|Passes empty string for etag (line 419), performing unconditional
- overwrite.  Same single-writer safety, same accidental passthrough
- possibility, same missed intentional protection.

+ response etag is extracted and asserted non-empty

-|Etag described as conditional

- {gcs_service} `getIamPolicy` always returns etag â€” the fallback

 |Secret Manager ({rbtoe_iam_grant_secret})
-|Resolved â€” now has propagation retry
-|The three secret IAM grants (formerly inline in {rbtgo_depot_levy})
- are now extracted into {rbtoe_iam_grant_secret} with full propagation

-|{rbtoe_iam_grant_repo}, {rbtoe_iam_grant_sa}, {rbtoe_iam_grant_bucket}
-|No requestedPolicyVersion in getIamPolicy

+|Full propagation retry, 409, non-empty etag
+|The three secret IAM grants (formerly inline in {rbtgo_depot_levy}) live
+ in `rbgi_grant_secret_iam` with full propagation retry per
+ <<rbscip_trade_study>>, GET getIamPolicy with
+ `options.requestedPolicyVersion=3`, asserted non-empty etag, POST SET,

+|{rbtoe_iam_grant_repo}, {rbtoe_iam_grant_sa}, {rbtoe_iam_grant_bucket}, {rbtoe_iam_grant_secret}

+ parameter for the GET sites, request body for the SA POST), matching

 |===
 
 ===== Convergence Analysis
 
-Based on these findings, the following convergence opportunities exist:
+**Converged (zero new state space â€” now realized in code):**
 
-**Safe to converge (zero new state space):**
+* **Etag extracted and passed intentionally**: Every API always returns etag.
+  Every grant pattern now extracts it explicitly, asserts it non-empty, and
+  passes it to `setIamPolicy` through the compose function â€” intentionally
+  concurrency-safe writes rather than accidental passthrough or unconditional
+  overwrite.  This also protects against Google-internal concurrent policy
+  modifications that the single-writer invariant does not cover.
 
-* **Always extract and pass etag intentionally**: Every API always returns etag.
-  Extracting it explicitly and passing it to `setIamPolicy` converts accidental
-  passthrough or unconditional overwrites to intentionally concurrency-safe
-  writes.  The compose function already handles etag correctly when provided.
-  This also protects against Google-internal concurrent policy modifications
-  that the single-writer invariant does not cover.
+* **Transient retry on SET (429/5xx)**: Every grant pattern's SET loop now
+  treats 429 (rate limit) and 5xx (server error) as transient and retries
+  within the eventual-consistency budget.
 
-* **Transient retry on SET (429/5xx)**: Currently only {rbtoe_iam_grant_project}
-  retries transient errors.  Adding bounded retry for 429 (rate limit) and 5xx
-  (server error) is universally correct and cannot cause new failures.
+* **Uniform etag-mismatch handling**: All standard IAM mixin APIs (CRM, IAM,
+  {gar_registry}, Secret Manager) die fatally on 409 ABORTED; {gcs_service}
+  dies fatally on 412 Precondition Failed (with a defensive 409 branch).
 
-* **Uniform etag-mismatch handling**: Handle 409 ABORTED for all standard IAM
-  mixin APIs, and 412 Precondition Failed for {gcs_service} only.  The current
-  project code's 412 handler should become a 409 handler.
-
-* **Request policy version 3 everywhere**: Protects against silent condition
-  destruction if conditioned policies are ever introduced on repo, SA, or
-  bucket resources.  For APIs that use GET (where version is a query param),
-  this is a query parameter addition; for POST APIs, it is a request body
-  field.
+* **Policy version 3 requested everywhere**: All five patterns request
+  version 3 â€” a query parameter on the GET sites
+  ({rbtoe_iam_grant_repo}, {rbtoe_iam_grant_secret}, {rbtoe_iam_grant_bucket})
+  and a request-body field on the POST sites
+  ({rbtoe_iam_grant_project}, {rbtoe_iam_grant_sa}) â€” closing the
+  silent-condition-destruction hazard on the resource-scope types.
 
 **Load-bearing differences (do not homogenize):**
 
@@ -477,10 +469,20 @@ Based on these findings, the following convergence opportunities exist:
 
 **Require investigation before converging** (tracked in <<rbshr_horizon_roadmap>>)**:**
 
-* **Read-back verification universalization**
-* **SA preflight universalization**
+* **SA preflight universalization** â€” {rbtoe_iam_grant_sa} does a hard
+  SA-existence GET preflight; whether the other resource-scope patterns warrant
+  an analogous preflight is open.
 * **SA numeric ID for member references**
 
+**Closed by design (not a convergence target):**
+
+* **Read-back verification** â€” the post-set read-back verify loop was removed
+  from {rbtoe_iam_grant_project} as non-load-bearing: the `setIamPolicy` 200 is
+  the authoritative grant confirmation, and the emailâ†’uid read reconciliation
+  could lag past any bounded poll and produce a false timeout on a grant that
+  already succeeded.  Universalizing read-back is therefore explicitly not a
+  direction; see the rationale comment in `rbgi_add_project_iam_role`.
+

 NOTE: Secondary network attachment is now managed declaratively by Docker Compose.
 The sentry's compose service definition lists both
 {at_transit_network}
-(priority 1000) and
-{at_enclave_network}
-(priority 500).
+and
+{at_enclave_network}.
 Compose attaches both networks when the container starts â€” there is no separate connect step.
 
-IMPORTANT: Docker Compose does not guarantee which network maps to which interface name (eth0, eth1).
-The compose `priority` field influences default route selection but does NOT control interface enumeration order.
-The sentry startup script discovers its interfaces by IP using `ip -o addr show to Â«enclave_ipÂ»` and never references hardcoded interface names.
+IMPORTANT: Docker Compose controls neither interface naming (eth0, eth1) nor default route selection.
+Compose `priority` keys are empirically NOT honored for route selection on linux Docker engine 28.x or mac Docker Desktop 28.x, and the compose file does not set them.
+Docker assigns the default route to the gateway of the alphabetically-first attached network, which is not necessarily the uplink.
+The load-bearing mechanism is sentry-side: the startup script discovers its interfaces by IP using `ip -o addr show` (never hardcoded interface names), derives the uplink gateway as the first usable address of the uplink subnet, and then replaces the default route via the uplink (`ip route replace default via Â«uplink_gatewayÂ»`) so egress leaves through transit rather than the enclave.
 
 //axhoc_completion
 After compose starts the sentry, it has connectivity on both networks with static IP on the enclave.

## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness â€” fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly â€” if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; highâ†’max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only â€” it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 â†’ 4.6, 4.6 â†’ 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch
